title: Factor pairs
description: Practice finding factor pairs for whole numbers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are factor pairs for $16$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A factor pair is $2$ whole numbers that can be multiplied to get a certain product.

In this case, we are looking for pairs that have a product of $\green{16}$.

The factor pairs for $\green{16}$ are: 

- $1$ and $16$  
- $2$ and $8$
- $4$ and $4$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are factor pairs for $66$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A factor pair is $2$ whole numbers that can be multiplied to get a certain product.

In this case, we are looking for pairs that have a product of $\green{66}$.

- $1 \times 66 = \green {66}$
- $2 \times 33 = \green{66}$
- $3 \times 22  = \green {66}$
- $4 \times 16= 64$
- $6 \times 11 = \green{66}$

The factor pairs for $\green{66}$ are: 

- $1$ and $66$  
- $2$ and $33$
- $3$ and $22$
- $6$ and $11$

assessment_items.question_content:
A rectangle has an area of $20$ square centimeters.  

**Which of the following could be the rectangle's length and width?**  
(Area $=$ length $\times$ width) 
  
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Area $=$ length $\times$ width

The area of the rectangle is $\purple{20}$ square centimeters. The length and width are a factor pair with a product of $\purple{20}$.

$1 \times 20 = \purple {20}$  
[[☃ image 1]]

$2 \times 10 = \purple {20}$   

[[☃ image 2]]

$4 \times 5 = \purple {20}$  
[[☃ image 3]]

[[☃ image 1]]

$3 \times 8 = 24$

The following factor pairs could be the rectangle's length and width:

- $1\text{ cm}$ and $20\text{ cm}$
- $2\text{ cm}$ and $10\text{ cm}$
- $4\text{ cm}$ and $5\text{ cm}$

assessment_items.question_content:
There are $56$ unicorns at magic camp.  The unicorns stay in large fenced-in fields.  Every unicorn is in a field, and each field has the same number of unicorns.

**Use what you know about factor pairs to complete the table.**

Number of fields| Unicorns per field
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $56$
$2$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $14$
$7$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
hints:
We know there are $\greenD{56 \text{ total unicorns}}$.

$\redD{\text{Number of fields}} \times \blueD{\text{ Number of unicorns per field}} = \greenD{\text{Total unicorns}}$

$\redD? \times\blueD{56} = \greenD{56}$  
$\redD1 \times\blueD{56} = \greenD{56}$   

If there is $\redD1$ field, there will be $\blueD{56}$ unicorns in the field.

$\redD2 \times{}\blueD? = \greenD{56}$  
$\redD2 \times\blueD{28} = \greenD{56}$   

If there are $\redD2$ fields, there will be $\blueD{28}$ unicorns in each field.

$\redD? \times\blueD{14} = \greenD{56}$  
$\redD4 \times\blueD{14} = \greenD{56}$   

If there are $\redD4$ fields, there will be $\blueD{14}$ unicorns in each field.

$\redD? \times\blueD8 = \greenD{56}$  
$\redD7 \times\blueD{8} = \greenD{56}$   

If there are $\redD7$ fields, there will be $\blueD{8}$ unicorns in each field.

Here is the completed table:

Number of fields| Unicorns per field
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $56$
$2$ | $28$
$4$ | $14$
$7$ |  $8$

assessment_items.question_content:
Blanca has $32$ marbles.  She wants to put her marbles into bags so each bag has the same number of marbles and no marbles are left over. 

**Use what you know about factor pairs to complete the table.**

Number of Bags | Marbles per bag
:-: | :-: 
$1$ |  [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $16$
$4$ |  [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
hints:
We know there are $\greenD{32 \text{ total marbles}}$.

$\redD{\text{number of bags}} \times \blueD{\text{ number of marbles per bag}} = \greenD{\text{total marbles}}$

$\redD1 \times{}\blueD? = \greenD{32}$  
$\redD1 \times\blueD{32} = \greenD{32}$  

If Blanca uses $\redD1$ bag, there will be $\blueD{32}$ marbles in the bag.

$\redD?  \times\blueD{16} = \greenD{32}$  
$\redD2 \times\blueD{16} = \greenD{32}$   

If Blanca uses $\redD2$ bags, there will be $\blueD{16}$ marbles in each bag.

$\redD4 \times{}\blueD? = \greenD{32}$  
$\redD4 \times\blueD{8} = \greenD{32}$   

If Blanca uses $\redD4$ bags, there will be $\blueD8$ marbles in each bag.

Here is the completed table:

Number of Bags | Marbles per bag
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ |  $32$
$2$ | $16$
$4$ |  $8$

assessment_items.question_content:
$40$ students are going on a field trip to the aquarium.  Every student needs to be in a group, and all groups must have the same number of students.

**Use what you know about factor pairs to complete the table.**

Number of groups | Students per group
:-: | :-: 
[[☃ numeric-input 1]] | $40$
$2$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
[[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $10$
hints:
We know there are $\greenD{40 \text{ total students}}$.

$\redD{\text{Number of groups}} \times \blueD{\text{ Number of students per group}} = \greenD{\text{Total students}}$

$\redD? \times\blueD{40} = \greenD{40}$  
$\redD1 \times\blueD{40} = \greenD{40}$   

If there is $\redD1$ group, there will be $\blueD{40}$ students in the group.

$\redD2  \times{}\blueD{?} = \greenD{40}$  
$\redD2 \times\blueD{20} = \greenD{40}$   

If there are $\redD2$  groups, there will be $\blueD{20}$ students in each group.

$\redD? \times\blueD{10} = \greenD{40}$  
$\redD4 \times\blueD{10} = \greenD{40}$   

If there are $\redD4$  groups, there will be $\blueD{10}$ students in each group.

Here is the completed table:

Number of groups | Students per group
- | - | -
$1$| $40$
$2$ | $20$
$4$ | $10$

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of $24$ friends is making teams to play tag.  Every player must be on a team, and each team must have the same number of players.  

**Use what you know about factor pairs to complete the table.**

Number of teams | Players per team
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $24$
$2$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $8$
$4$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
hints:
We know there are $\greenD{24 \text{ total players}}$.

$\redD{\text{Number of teams}} \times \blueD{\text{ Number of players per team}} = \greenD{\text{Total players}}$

$\redD2  \times{} \blueD{?} = \greenD{24}$  
$\redD2 \times \blueD{12} = \greenD{24}$   

If there are $\redD2$ teams, there will be $\blueD{12}$ players on each team.

$\redD? \times\blueD{8} = \greenD{24}$  
$\redD3 \times\blueD{8} = \greenD{24}$   

If there are $\redD3$  teams, there will be $\blueD{8}$ players on each team.

$\redD4 \times{}\blueD{?} = \greenD{24}$  
$\redD4 \times\blueD{6} = \greenD{24}$   

If there are $\redD4$ teams, there will be $\blueD{6}$ players on each team.

Here is the completed table:

Number of teams | Players per team
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $24$
$2$ | $12$
$3$| $8$
$4$ | $6$

assessment_items.question_content:
Tyler is cleaning up his $36$ toy cars.  He wants to put every car in a toy bin, and he wants each bin to have the same number of cars.

**Use what you know about factor pairs to complete the table.**

Number of bins | Cars per bin
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $36$
$2$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $12$
$4$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
$6$ | $6$
hints:
We know there are $\green{36 \text{ total cars}}$.

$\redD{\text{Number of bins}} \times \blueD{\text{ Number of cars per bin}} = \greenD{\text{Total number of cars}}$

$\redD2 \times{}\blueD? = \greenD{36}$  
$\redD2 \times\blueD{18} = \greenD{36}$   

If there are $\redD2$ bins, there will be $\blueD{18}$ cars in each bin.

$\redD? \times\blueD{12} = \greenD{36}$  
$\redD3 \times\blueD{12} = \greenD{36}$   

If there are $\redD3$ bins, there will be $\blueD{12}$ cars in each bin.

$\redD4 \times{}\blueD? = \greenD{36}$  
$\redD4 \times\blueD{9} = \greenD{36}$   

If there are $\redD4$ bins, there will be $\blueD{9}$ cars in each bin.

Here is the completed table:

Number of bins | Cars per bin
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $36$
$2$ | $18$
$3$ | $12$
$4$ | $9$
$6$ | $6$

assessment_items.question_content:
Davi has $75$ flower seeds.  He wants to plant the seeds in pots so that every seed is planted, and every pot has the same number of seeds.  

**Use what you know about factor pairs to complete the table.**

Number of pots | Seeds per pot
:-: | :-: 
 [[☃ numeric-input 1]] | $75$
$3$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]] 
 [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $15$
hints:
We know there are $\greenD{75 \text{ total seeds}}$.

$\redD{\text{Number of pots}} \times \blueD{\text{ Number of seeds per pot}} = \greenD{\text{Total number of seeds}}$

$\redD? \times\blueD{75} = \greenD{75}$  
$\redD1 \times\blueD{75} = \greenD{75}$   

If there is $\redD1$ pot, there will be $\blueD{75}$ seeds in the pot.

$\redD3 \times{}\blueD? = \greenD{75}$  
$\redD3 \times\blueD{25} = \greenD{75}$   

If there are $\redD3$ pots, there will be $\blueD{25}$ seeds in each pot.

$\redD? \times\blueD{15} = \greenD{75}$  
$\redD5 \times\blueD{15} = \greenD{75}$   

If there are $\redD5$ pots, there will be $\blueD{15}$ seeds in each pot.

Here is the completed table:

Number of pots | Seeds per pot
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $75$
$3$ | $25$
$5$ | $15$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the table to find all the factor pairs of $88$.**

First factor | | Second factor
:-: | - | :-:
$1$ | and | $88$
$2$ | and | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | and | $22$
[[☃ numeric-input 3]] | and | $11$
hints: A factor pair is $2$ whole numbers that we can multiply to get a certain product.

In this case, we are looking for pairs that have a product of $\greenE{88}$.  

$\begin{align}
1 \times 88 &= \greenE{88}\\\\
2 \times 44 &= \greenE{88}\\\\
4 \times 22 &= \greenE{88}\\\\
8 \times 11 &= \greenE{88}
\end{align}$

Here is the completed table:

First factor | | Second factor
:-: | - | :-:
$1$ | and | $88$
$2$ | and | $\blueE{44}$
$\blueE{4}$ | and | $22$
$\blueE{8}$ | and | $11$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the table to find all the factor pairs of $30$.**

First factor | | Second factor
:-: | - | :-:
[[☃ numeric-input 4]] | and | $30$
$2$ | and | $15$
$3$ | and | [[☃ numeric-input 5]]
[[☃ numeric-input 6]] | and | $6$
hints: A factor pair is $2$ whole numbers that we can multiply to get a certain product.

In this case, we are looking for pairs that have a product of $\greenE{30}$.  

$\begin{align}
1 \times 30 &= \greenE{30}\\\\
2 \times 15 &= \greenE{30}\\\\
3 \times 10 &= \greenE{30}\\\\
5 \times 6 &= \greenE{30}
\end{align}$

Here is the completed table:

First factor | | Second factor
:-: | - | :-:
$\blueE{1}$ | and | $30$
$2$ | and | $15$
$3$ | and | $\blueE{10}$
$\blueE{5}$ | and | $6$
